text.content: "In the last lesson, you built a rectangle with 4 square tiles in each row and 5 rows. You checked it and saw that the area was 20 square tiles. Today you'll look at that same rectangle again and learn two different ways to write what you already know about it. The equal sign is the key. Let's look at what it really means."
Hundred Grid Mode B appears with canonical 4 × 5 rectangle filled. 20 square tiles visible. Build affordances disabled — student sees rectangle but cannot place tiles.
"Here is the rectangle you built last lesson. It has 4 square tiles in each row and 5 rows. The area is 20 square tiles."

tool: check  target: hundred_grid_mode_b
"Tap Check to continue."
✅ Student tapped Check, Mode C activates — student moves forward
⏭️ Student presses Next

Mode C activates. Two-panel strip appears below rectangle: left panel shows 4 × 5 = 20, right panel shows 20 = 4 × 5. Bridge element (double-headed arrow or =) between panels signals equivalence. Both panels identical font size, background, visual weight.
Static balance-scale asset appears in tutor explanation zone alongside Mode C strip. Shows balance beam with equal weight on both sides.
"Look at the equal sign. It's not a go button. It's a balance. What's on this side has the same value as what's on that side. The equal sign means both sides have the same value."
⏭️ Student presses Next — toggle open to see current scene state
Hundred Grid Mode C active. Rectangle visible above. Two-panel strip below: left panel 4 × 5 = 20, right panel 20 = 4 × 5, bridge element between. Tap-and-hold enabled on panels.
Balance-scale asset visible in tutor zone.
"Tap and hold each panel. See what happens."

tool: tap_and_hold  target: hundred_grid_mode_b.mode_c_panels
"Tap and hold the left panel, then the right panel."
✅ Student tapped and held both panels, observed rectangle highlighting
Both panels cross-highlight rectangle when tapped. Same rectangle highlights for both equations.
"Right. Both equations point to the same rectangle. They have the same value."
❌ Student selected 'the answer goes on the right' — computational misconception
Both panels highlight to show symmetric relationship.
"Look at both panels. Each one shows the same rectangle. The equal sign does not point to an answer. It shows that both sides have the same value."
❌ Student selected 'I should multiply' — operation cue misconception
Balance scale asset highlights to reinforce balance metaphor.
"The equal sign is not telling you what to do. It is showing a balance. What is on this side has the same value as what is on that side."
❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.
Both panels animate to highlight the same rectangle, demonstrating equivalence.

"Look at what happens when I tap each panel. Both point to the same rectangle. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. That is what makes it a balance. The correct answer is both sides have the same value."
⏭️ Student presses Next
Fill-in-the-blank stem appears: The equal sign means ___. Three options visible.
"Now finish the sentence. The equal sign means what?"

tool: fill_in_blank  target: fill_in_blank_stem
"Select the correct option."
✅ Student selected both sides have the same value, correct
"You got it. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. It's a balance, not a go button."
Hundred Grid Mode B appears displaying a 4 × 5 rectangle (4 columns, 5 rows, 20 square tiles total). Build affordances are disabled — rectangle is static reference visual only.
Multiple Choice / Checkbox interface appears showing four equation candidates.
"Look at this rectangle. You know it shows 4 times 5 equals 20. But there's more than one way to write that fact."

tool: multi_select  target: equation_checkbox  options: ["4 × 5 = 20", "20 = 4 × 5", "4 + 5 = 20", "4 × 20 = 5"]
"Choose ALL the equations that correctly describe this rectangle."
✅ Student selected both correct equations: 4 × 5 = 20 and 20 = 4 × 5
"That's right. Both of those describe the same rectangle. Two ways to write the same fact."
❌ Branch 2: Student selected one correct equation but not both (under-selecting)
Rectangle highlights to draw attention to the area.
"You chose one correct equation. Look at the rectangle again. Both equations describe this same rectangle. Are there any other equations that show the same value?"
❌ Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect equations (all-wrong)
Rectangle highlights to draw attention.
"This rectangle shows multiplication. Count the columns: 4. Count the rows: 5. The total area is 20 square tiles. Which equations use multiplication to show that fact?"
❌ Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect equations (over-selecting)
Rectangle highlights.
"You found equations that match the rectangle. But one or more of your choices don't fit. Look at the operations in each equation. Does the rectangle show addition, or does it show multiplication?"
❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.
System highlights columns (4) and rows (5), then pulses total area (20).
Correct equations highlight in the checkbox interface.

"This rectangle has 4 columns and 5 rows. That means 4 times 5 equals 20. You can write that as 4 × 5 = 20 or as 20 = 4 × 5. Both equations describe the same rectangle because the equal sign means both sides have the same value."
⏭️ Student presses Next
Drop Down / Fill-in-the-Blank prompt appears with completion stem.
"Now finish this sentence about why both equations work."

tool: fill_in_blank  target: drop_down_stem  options: ["they show two ways to write the same fact", "the equal sign means both sides have the same value", "one of them must be wrong"]
"These equations are correct because ___."
✅ Student selected first reinforcing option
"Exactly. Two ways to write the same fact. The equal sign lets you write multiplication either way."
✅ Student selected second reinforcing option
"That's it. Both sides have the same value. That's what the equal sign tells you."
❌ Student selected one of them must be wrong on first attempt
"Look again at what the equal sign means. Does it mean one side is right and the other is wrong?"
❌ Student selected one of them must be wrong on second attempt
Rectangle highlights to reinforce reference visual.
"The equal sign means both sides have the same value. Both equations are correct because they both describe this rectangle. Which answer says that?"
❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.
Rectangle pulses to show the area value.
Correct option highlights in the fill-in-the-blank interface.

"Both equations are correct because they show two ways to write the same fact. 4 × 5 = 20 and 20 = 4 × 5 both describe this rectangle. The equal sign means both sides have the same value, so you can write the equation either way."
Hundred Grid Mode B displays a 4 × 5 rectangle: 4 columns, 5 rows, 20 total tiles.
Mode C paired-equation display appears below rectangle. Left panel shows 4 × 5 = 20. Right panel shows 4 × 5 = 5 × 4.
"Both of these equations describe the same rectangle."

tool: multiple_choice  options: ["4 × 5 = 5 × 4", "4 × 5 = 5 + 4"]
"Tap the one that says it correctly."
✅ Student selected 4 × 5 = 5 × 4, the correct multiplicative-commutative form
"You got it."
❌ Student selected 4 × 5 = 5 + 4, confusing multiplication with addition
Left panel 4 × 5 = 20 highlights.
"Look at the left panel. 4 × 5 means 4 times 5, not 4 plus 5. Multiplication and addition are different operations. Which equation uses multiplication on both sides?"
❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.
Right panel 4 × 5 = 5 × 4 highlights and pulses to confirm it is the correct equation.

"The correct equation is 4 × 5 = 5 × 4. Both sides use multiplication. 4 times 5 equals 20, and 5 times 4 also equals 20. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. That is why this equation is correct."
⏭️ Student presses Next
Drop Down prompt appears with stem and three options.
"Complete this sentence."

tool: fill_in_blank  target: drop_down_stem
"Both sides of 4 × 5 = 5 × 4 show the ___."
✅ Student selected same value, correct completion
"Right. Both sides of 4 × 5 = 5 × 4 show the same value, 20. The equal sign means same value on both sides, no matter how many operations are on each side."
❌ Student selected same number of square tiles only, focusing on physical tiles rather than value concept
Rectangle pulses to show it represents a value, not just tiles.
"The square tiles help us see the value. But both sides show the same value, 20. What does the equal sign tell us about the two sides?"
❌ Student selected same number of rows only, focusing on one dimension rather than overall value
Both panels pulse to emphasize the complete equations, not just parts.
"The number of rows is part of each side. But the equal sign connects the whole side to the whole other side. What does it mean when both sides have the same total?"
❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.
Both panels highlight and a connection animates between them showing 20 = 20.

"Both sides of 4 × 5 = 5 × 4 show the same value. The left side equals 20. The right side also equals 20. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. That is the key idea about what the equal sign tells us."
Hundred Grid Mode B displays a 4 × 5 rectangle. 4 columns, 5 rows, 20 total tiles.
Mode C paired display shows both equation orientations. Left panel: 4 × 5 = 20. Right panel: 20 = 4 × 5. Equivalence bridge connects them.
Drop Down stem unlocks with six numeric slots. First blank set: 4, 5, 20. Second blank set: 20, 4, 5. Both equation orientations required.
"This rectangle lets you write the same fact in two ways. Finish the sentence using the numbers from both panels."

tool: fill_in_blank  target: drop_down_stem
"Fill in the six slots to write both equations."
✅ Student filled all six slots correctly: 4 × 5 = 20 AND 20 = 4 × 5
"You wrote both of them. 4 × 5 = 20 and 20 = 4 × 5. Same fact, two ways. The equal sign means both sides have the same value."
❌ Student filled slots incorrectly on first attempt
"Look at the panels. What numbers do you see in 4 × 5 = 20?"
❌ Student filled slots incorrectly on second attempt
Both panels highlight to draw attention to the numbers.
"Write the left panel first: 4 × 5 = 20. Then write the right panel: 20 = 4 × 5. Use the exact numbers you see."
❌ Student filled slots incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.
System fills slots one by one: 4, 5, 20, then 20, 4, 5. Both equations appear in order.
"The first three slots write 4 × 5 = 20. The next three write 20 = 4 × 5. Both equations describe the same rectangle. The equal sign means both sides have the same value."
